questions.to_find: The output printed by the program involving struct pointer arithmetic

understand.plain: The program defines an array of structs, each holding an int and a char pointer. A pointer p walks through the array using pointer arithmetic. The key operations are: incrementing the struct pointer (p += 1 moves to next struct), and incrementing a char pointer within a struct (++p->c moves one character forward inside a string). We must trace each step to determine what gets printed.

keywords.explain: p += 1 on a struct pointer moves p to the next struct in the array (not just one byte).
keywords.example: If st[0] is at address 0, st[1] is at address sizeof(struct test).

keywords.explain: Due to precedence, -> binds tighter than ++. So this is ++(p->c), which increments the char* field c inside the struct that p points to, advancing it past the first character.
keywords.example: If p->c points to 'better', after ++p->c it points to 'etter'.

keywords.explain: p++ -> c first accesses p->c (using current p), then increments p to point to the next struct.
keywords.example: If p points to st[1], p++ -> c gives st[1].c, then p becomes st[2].

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='150' height='60' class='bg'/><text x='85' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>st[0]</text><text x='85' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>i=5, c->"become"</text><rect x='10' y='80' width='150' height='60' class='hi'/><text x='85' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>st[1] (p after p+=1)</text><text x='85' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>i=4, c->"etter" (after ++c)</text><rect x='10' y='150' width='150' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='85' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>st[2] (p after p++)</text><text x='200' y='105' class='lbl ink'>p+=1 -> st[1]</text><text x='200' y='130' class='sub mut'>++p->c: "better"->"etter"</text><text x='200' y='155' class='sub mut'>printf: "etter"</text><text x='200' y='175' class='sub mut'>p++ -> now st[2]</text><text x='200' y='55' class='sub mut'>i=6, c->"jungle"</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Array of structs st[0..4]. p starts at st[0], moves to st[1] via p+=1. ++p->c changes c in st[1] from 'better' to 'etter'. printf prints 'etter' and advances p to st[2] (i=6, c='jungle').

given.aim: Trace struct pointer operations to determine the printed output.

terms.meaning: Array of struct test initialized: st[0]={5,"become"}, st[1]={4,"better"}, st[2]={6,"jungle"}, st[3]={8,"ancestor"}, st[4]={7,"brother"}
terms.connects: The data values that will be read and printed

terms.meaning: Moves struct pointer p from st[0] to st[1]
Increments the char* field c in the struct p points to; 'better' becomes 'etter'
terms.example: String pointer advances past 'b', pointing at 'etter'
terms.connects: Determines what the first printf outputs

terms.meaning: Returns p->c (current value 'etter') and then increments p to st[2]
terms.example: First print uses st[1].c = 'etter'; p now = st[2]
terms.connects: After the first printf, p points to st[2] = {6, 'jungle'}

given.plan: Step through each statement: (1) p = st -> st[0]. (2) p += 1 -> st[1]. (3) ++p->c increments st[1].c: 'better' -> 'etter'. (4) printf("%s,", p++->c) prints 'etter' and moves p to st[2]. (5) printf("%d,", p->i) prints st[2].i = 6. (6) printf("%s", p->c) prints st[2].c = 'jungle'.

steps.title: Initialize and advance pointer
steps.apply: p starts at st[0] = {5, 'become'}. After p += 1, p points to st[1] = {4, 'better'}.
steps.note: Pointer arithmetic on a struct pointer advances by sizeof(struct test), not by 1 byte.

steps.title: Increment char pointer inside struct
steps.apply: p->c currently points to the string 'better'. After ++p->c, p->c points to the character 'e', so the string now reads as 'etter'.
steps.note: Operator precedence: -> has higher precedence than prefix ++, so this modifies the char* field, not the struct pointer.

steps.title: First printf: print p->c then post-increment p
steps.apply: p->c is 'etter'. printf prints 'etter,'. Then p is post-incremented to st[2] = {6, 'jungle'}.
steps.note: Post-increment returns the old value (p = st[1]) for the current expression, then advances p.

steps.title: Second and third printf
steps.apply: p now points to st[2] = {6, 'jungle'}. Second printf prints '6,'. Third printf prints 'jungle'.
steps.note: p was not incremented between these two printfs, so both use st[2].

solution.result: Output is: etter,6,jungle — matching option C.

questions.formula_note: Struct pointer arithmetic and operator precedence (-> binds tighter than prefix ++; post-increment vs prefix increment on pointers)

questions.answer:
C. Choice A
   Choice BNo Choice
questions.to_find: The output of the C switch statement with no break statements (fall-through behavior)

understand.plain: In C, a switch statement executes the matching case and then continues executing all subsequent cases unless a break statement stops it. This is called fall-through. Since no break is present anywhere in this switch, once case 'A' matches, execution will continue through all following cases including default, printing everything.

keywords.explain: When a switch case has no break, execution continues into the next case label and runs that code too.
keywords.example: case 'A' runs, then falls through to case 'B'/'C', then to 'D'/'E'/default.

keywords.explain: A case label with no code (like case 'B':) simply serves as an additional entry point; execution falls through to the next non-empty case.
keywords.example: case 'B': and case 'C': are grouped, so both 'B' and 'C' would trigger the same printf.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='400' height='260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='180' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='100' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>case 'A' MATCH</text><line x1='100' y1='50' x2='100' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arrow)'/><rect x='10' y='80' width='180' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>print "Choice A\n"</text><line x1='100' y1='120' x2='100' y2='150' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='140' class='sub mut'>no break -> fall through</text><rect x='10' y='150' width='180' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>print "Choice B"</text><line x1='100' y1='190' x2='100' y2='220' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='210' class='sub mut'>no break -> fall through</text><rect x='10' y='220' width='180' height='35' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='243' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>print "No Choice"</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Flow diagram: case 'A' matches and prints 'Choice A', then falls through (no break) to print 'Choice B', then falls through again to print 'No Choice'.

given.aim: Determine the output when a switch with no break statements is executed for inChar = 'A'.

terms.meaning: The switch expression evaluates to 'A', so execution begins at case 'A'.
terms.connects: Entry point into the switch body.

terms.meaning: After printing 'Choice A\n', execution does not exit; it falls through to case 'B' and then case 'C'.
terms.example: Both case 'B' and 'C' share the printf("Choice B") statement.
terms.connects: Causes 'Choice B' to also be printed.

terms.meaning: Execution falls through from case 'C' through empty cases 'D' and 'E' to default, printing 'No Choice'.
terms.example: default executes even though the value matched case 'A', due to fall-through.

given.plan: Trace execution from case 'A' all the way to default since no break is present anywhere. Accumulate all printf outputs.

steps.title: Match case 'A' and execute
steps.apply: inChar = 'A' matches case 'A'. printf prints 'Choice A' followed by a newline. No break follows, so execution falls through.
steps.note: The \n causes a newline after 'Choice A'.

steps.title: Fall through case 'B', execute case 'C' printf
steps.apply: case 'B' is empty, execution continues to case 'C' which has printf("Choice B"). This prints 'Choice B' (no newline). No break, falls through again.
steps.note: case 'B' has no code but does not block fall-through from case 'A'.

steps.title: Fall through cases 'D', 'E', default
steps.apply: cases 'D' and 'E' are empty. Execution reaches default and prints 'No Choice'. No more code; switch ends.
steps.note: Output so far: 'Choice A\nChoice BNo Choice'.

solution.result: Output: Choice A (newline) Choice BNo Choice — matching option C.

questions.formula_note: Switch fall-through in C: without a break statement, execution continues sequentially through all subsequent case labels

questions.to_find: The total number of times a printf statement executes in the program

understand.plain: This program computes j from a mixed integer/float expression, then derives k using a compound assignment. A for loop runs 5 times, and inside each iteration a switch selects among two cases based on i+k. Since there are no break statements, fall-through can cause multiple printfs in one iteration. We count total printf calls across all iterations.

keywords.explain: 2*3/4 is computed as (2*3)/4 = 6/4 = 1 (integer division). 8/5 = 1. But 2.0/5 = 0.4 (floating point), and since j is int, the whole expression is truncated to an integer.
--j decrements j first (j becomes 1), then k -= j means k = k - j = 0 - 1 = -1.
When case 1 matches and has no break, execution continues into case 2 as well, causing two printfs in one iteration.
keywords.example: When i+k=1, both case 1 and case 2 printf execute.

understand.visual_alt: Table of i values (0-4), i+k values (-1,0,1,2,3), and printf count per iteration (0,0,2,1,0). Total = 3.

given.aim: Count the total number of printf executions in the loop.

terms.meaning: Integer expression evaluation: (2*3)/4 = 1, 2.0/5 = 0.4, 8/5 = 1. Sum = 2.4, truncated to j = 2.
terms.example: j is int, so the fractional part 0.4 is discarded.
terms.connects: Initial value of j before k computation.

terms.meaning: --j decrements j from 2 to 1; then k = k - j = 0 - 1 = -1.
terms.connects: k=-1 shifts the switch expression in the loop.

terms.meaning: switch(i - 1). Only matches case 1 (when i=2) and case 2 (when i=3).
terms.example: i=2: i+k=1 (case 1 match); i=3: i+k=2 (case 2 match).

terms.meaning: When case 1 matches (i=2), execution falls through to case 2 as well, causing 2 printfs.
terms.example: i=2 triggers case 1 then falls into case 2: 2 printfs total for that iteration.
terms.connects: Adds an extra printf when case 1 is matched.

given.plan: Compute j and k. Then for each i in [0,4], compute i+k and determine how many case labels fire (accounting for fall-through). Sum all printf calls.

steps.title: Compute j
steps.apply: 2*3 = 6; 6/4 = 1 (integer division). 2.0/5 = 0.4 (float). 8/5 = 1 (integer division). Sum = 1 + 0.4 + 1 = 2.4. Since j is int, j = 2 (truncation).
steps.note: Mixed int and float arithmetic: only 2.0/5 produces a float; the final assignment to int truncates.

steps.title: Compute k
steps.apply: --j: j goes from 2 to 1. k -= j means k = 0 - 1 = -1. After: j=1, k=-1.
steps.note: k -= --j is NOT k -= j--. The pre-decrement happens before the subtraction.

steps.title: Trace the loop iterations
steps.apply:
i=0: switch(-1) -> no match -> 0 printfs.
i=1: switch(0) -> no match -> 0 printfs.
i=2: switch(1) -> matches case 1, prints 'j=1'; no break, falls to case 2, prints 'j=1'. 2 printfs.
i=3: switch(2) -> matches case 2, prints 'j=1'. 1 printf.
i=4: switch(3) -> no match -> 0 printfs.
steps.note: i=2 causes 2 prints due to fall-through from case 1 to case 2.

steps.title: Total count
steps.note: j prints as 1 in all cases since --j made j=1 before the loop.

solution.result: The printf statement is executed 3 times.

questions.formula_note: C integer arithmetic truncation, pre-decrement operator precedence, and switch fall-through (no break causes execution to continue into subsequent case labels)
